item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the closest source (here) shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.
Plot twist: SCP-1839 was thrown at this spot and that spot is a fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is to say, you can magically turn all the fish in SCP-2025 into humans by altering objective reality all you want to, it wouldn't affect the perceptions or opinions of us bigoted humans.

Yes. Hence the statement in the SCP.
To break down the logic:
A is a fish.
B is a person who is aware of A's existence.
B does not believe that fish are humans.
A is placed inside SCP-2025.
When B thinks about A, they will consider A to be a human.
They will remain aware that A is a fish.
They will still believe that fish are not humans.
But when A is inside 2025, it is defined as human, independent of perceptions and opinions.
B is not affected by 2025; A has been changed in some way that alters its semantic definition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2025 is moving under his own power. Therefore he's human. We have to save him from disappearing under the sea floor! (Who's also human, due to seismic activity.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I have two main problems with this:

For these reasons, SCP-2025 is believed to encompass the underlying reality within SCP-2025-A, rather than simply influencing the perceptions of subjects.

Since when, ever, in the history of humaity, has the existence of an underlying reality in one place automatically changed the opinions of individuals in another place? If SCP-2025 really affected the "underlying reality" within SCP-2025-A, we wouldn't notice it. That is to say, you can magically turn all the fish in SCP-2025 into humans by altering objective reality all you want to, it wouldn't affect the perceptions or opinions of us bigoted humans. SCP-2025's most notable feature is that it only influences opinions/attitudes, and it does so at any tested range. Objective reality doesn't enter into it.

The other problem I have with this is that SCP-2025 itself is both entirely contained within SCP-2025 and has been observed as changing size relative to the Earth. That means it's moving. Why is SCP-2025 not itself classified as a humanoid SCP?

Yes I realize this hasn't been relevant since 2014!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[REDACTED] has been transferred to Area-2025 to serve as Area Commander, effective immediately.

Area Commander Ms. Mark Twain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one was somewhat disturbing:

… Self-professed "dog-lover." … Subject then stood, said "Well, if we're going to be alone for a while," and approached restrained subject. Using the restraints, D-0930 began [REDACTED], (since confirmed to be an approximation of his previous modus operandi).

I'm uncertain how I feel about the SCP in general.
No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A year and a half later, I love this. Just thought I'd let you know. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna catch hell for doing this
But it is totally worth it

Bees. Pie is bees. Dog is bees. Camera is made of bees.

Except here, everything is human.

I really like o0s metatag analogy upthread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'd need either a non-anomalous item that only works for humans, or a majority of O5s to agree to cross-testing at this point. It's easier from an economical, safety and "we got it" standpoint to deal with the effect and find command staff for the area that understand going in what it does.

Why do you think they kept getting veterinary and technical people instead of the items that were requested? My issue with that is people on the supply side could read this article and interpret any communications coming from inside the area of effect.

I agree with Mortte; the SCP should be the area it occupies, not the effect. Those are traditionally -1 or -A items (subset of overall SCP).

This is still screwing with my head the morning after reading it. +1, great concept.

Edit: Crosstesting would never be requested; they're already human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like that's missing the point. The idea is that the SCP's effects are metaphysical rather than real physical changes. Things affected don't actually become human, they just become classified as human. I interpret the metaphysical aspect as something like metadata on a computer. If I were to alter the metadata of an image file and say, change the name of the author/creator to someone else, the main contents of the photo would not change, you would still see the same photo. However, anything relying on the metadata to determine who created the image would be fooled into thinking it was someone else. Similarly, this thing somehow messes with the universe metaphysically to make these things seem like humans without actually changing anything physically about the objects affected. Even though their intelligence, behavior, and all physical aspects remain the same, people think they're human because their "metadata" says they're human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Not sure how I feel about this. I feel like there are some conceptual inconsistencies I can't put my finger on, and there's something that bugs me about the execution…

But I find this to be absolutely hilarious. No vote.

—-

Ah, what the heck. Upvote. Needs polish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's been a few months, but just in case you're still working on it (and I hope you are, because I think it's a very compelling idea and absolutely worth putting the work in to make it live up to its potential) I thought I'd add my two cents.

I think the issue with this is that it just isn't being conveyed in the article itself. It never explains why the Foundation considers the area of effect sinking to be an urgent problem nor why they believe drilling is the solution. One of the toughest things in writing is discerning between what you know in your head and what is actually being communicated on the page, and I think that accounts for some of the article's problems. Another problem in this vein I think is that (as others have pointed out) it's unclear if it imbues these things with humanity or just makes people see these things as human. The article says the former but seems to show the latter. (In a related issue, I wouldn't say the SCP is the concept of humanity since the concept of humanity exists outside this anomalous area of effect, which I think is really what the SCP is.)

Those are my two main issues. Other than that, so far, I think a lot of the feedback has been dead-on, and I hope you continue to work on this really intriguing SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure about this thing's effect.
Does it simply make humans think that animate non-human objects are actually human? Or does it make these animate non-human objects think they are human?
I assume it's the former, but I'm not entirely certain.

Also, why are they bothering with drilling down into the mantle? From the article I just assume the researchers on-site are doing it to 'protect' their 'human' friends or something. The foundation doesn't even seem to know if this will prevent the area of effect from sinking further into the earth and trying to maintain access to it is a highly implausible thing in the long run.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because, they can't tell what's actually human and what's actually not-human.

Imagine you wake up one day and everything looks like pie.
The doors are pie, your bed is pie, the walls, floor, ceiling is pie. Everything is pie!
You can't tell what's pie and what's not. There is no discernible difference between pie and not-pie. To your knowledge, everything is pie now.
EVERYTHING IS PIE!

Or, at least, that's what I'm getting from this. I could be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the 2000 contest, one of the more interesting SCPs in the 2000 Series. I respect your doubts, author, but well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even after reading the article and comments I'm still not entirely sure what's happening here. This one just doesn't click with me I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does make sense. I didn't really read that urgency in the article itself but I can definitely see that line of thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since several others have brought this up, I'll address this. My intent with it sinking has always been to provide a reason for the urgency behind Foundation actions.

It can be argued that the foundation would still drill if it wasn't sinking, but even at foundation tech-levels, drilling nearly into the mantle is prohibitively dangerous/expensive. So much so that, in my mind, the foundation would prefer to simply cordon off this remote, anomalous region rather than spend the money and expose personnel to its effects.

If the source is escaping, sinking into a more fluid part of the earth, it could either be destroyed or resurface sometime way down the road, except influencing a different, perhaps populated part of the world. Either result would be bad for the Foundation's mission.

That's my thinking anyway. I hope it makes sense to people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. Reading through the containment procedures and description gives you a nice "huh… okay." but it doesn't really sink in until the tests. And I think the tests were very well done. I think you could keep the source sinking part or not. I don't think it's 100% necessary to the impact of the article. I like the confusion of requesting new people and equipment (instead of the "humans" they were getting) but I think you could have them drilling for the source even if it's not sinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this is pre- or post-rewrite, but I really like it. The core concept of identity confusion is one that I really appreciate: it's intriguing and mind-warping, and also both horrific when you think about it and possessing great potential for humour.

I liked the interesting lead-in with the special mention of the working conditions. That made me intrigued to read the rest of the article - which is what good containment procedures should do. I particularly liked Ms. Twain, as well. That made me go and upvote before I even finished the article, which is not something i typically do.

I'm not sure about making it sink, though, as it feels a little bit like injecting artificial excitement. The Foundation could be just as easily employing deep-water drilling humans just to see what's under the crust.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But it is absolutely not memetic in any way, shape, or form. "Memetic" has a specific meaning, and it's much more narrow than "stuff what fucks with your brain". Understanding Memetics gives a much better explanation than I can put in this comment field.

As for being able to demonstrate that it gives things the metaphysical property of "humanity" as opposed to just being a cognitohazard, there's plenty of SCPs out there that only work when operated by a human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh MAN i did not know about 1539. That pulled off a very similar concept incredibly well.

As i rewrite, i will take all that into account and try to make mine unique. Thank you for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather like this one. Yeah, it's got a bit of an SCP-1539 thing going, but I liked 1539 too. If you do a little more with what this is like from the subjects' POV, it could be excellent. What Soulless pointed out would be an important point to tackle.

I'd encourage you to go even farther than that, though: what is this SCP like for a nonliving animate object? Drones and drill platforms become conceptually human, sure, but all that means for sure is that other sentient/sapient beings perceive and treat them as human. Does conceptual humanity convey sentience, sapience, or capacity for language (especially given that there exist humans who lack one or all of those qualities)? How exactly does a drilling platform go about complaining of inhumane working conditions? Or is that a delusion on the part of the human-humans working with it?

I'm upvoting this even in its current form; as it is, I like it just enough to do so (and besides I'd like to see it stick around long enough for a rewrite). If you improve this as much as I think you can, though, that'll change to an enthusiastic +1.

e: Also, you should definitely keep the photo. Its use of censorship and its caption are quite the little hook.

item.title: WELP. Almost time to pull the plug.
item.content:encoded:
I am beginning a rewrite and total revamp of this idea. I believe it has merit but obviously my execution was not as good as I thought it was. If this page does not fall far enough to be eligible for deletion within the next week, I will delete it. Obviously if it does fall that far before next week, I will watch it go without remorse. Okay, well… I'll shed a single tear. A big, slightly phlegmy tear.

The reason I'm not just immediately deleting it is in the hopes of getting more constructive feedback. I hope to salvage this idea, and I will get a wider array of feedback before posting next time. If, by some miracle, I can make this rewrite unquestionably good within the next week, I will simply edit this article. Otherwise I will only repost once it's good enough. I'm not terribly attached to the number 2025, so it's no big if I lose this slot.

So please, if you have any ideas that would make this work better, let me know. If you think the idea is bankrupt, let me know.

E: Well it's in the positives, so i'll hold off on deletion. Rewrite still underway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're trying to go for, but this is a little too esoteric for my tastes. Screwing with concept space is all well and good, but the way the Foundation pokes at it seems to suggest that they are both exempt and not exempt from the effect; testing lab rats that are clearly lab-rats and subject to that level of ethical consideration, and then burying them at sea like real people, for example.

Nah, not for me. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We contain.
We protect.
But we can't do either of those if we don't secure first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second that. The idea is quite good and leads to some funny wordings and subtle hints, but there are a few details that confuse it a bit. And I did not get why the Foundation was attempting to drill to prevent the SCP for sinking into the crust, apart from allowing the joke with drillmen complaining of the inhumane conditions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although it is a bit daunting at first (to wrap your head around that is), I think this is a good idea overall, albeit could use some polishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's an areas where people think animals and things are "humanity" then?

+ I'm having a real hard time wrapping my head around it
- Title for hiding text

Sprague-Dawley lab humans.

Had to google Sprague-Dawley to understand that. Why is it again that the researchers are referring to the rats as humans? Shouldn't they know they are not, or at least be able to go back and change that to be correct?

I'm still unclear about the whole, "abstract concept humanity" thing. I don't see why they wouldn't just assume this thing/area/ship, makes you think things are human when they are not. Much like the one scip that makes people think lamps are people.

It's a really thick read for me personally, and I have to go back through it several times to get what is being said/described.

The nature of SCP-2025 is such that any animate object within SCP-2025-A (including but not limited to humans, amphibians, [REDACTED], and remote controlled drones)

Why is there a redacted in there and why are we clarifying it includes humans and amphibians ?

Note: D-0854, SD-0577, SD-0578 have been buried at sea in accordance with Foundation "Disposal of Human Cadaver" guidelines.

Not sure it really is necessary to not what happened to him. D-Class tend to get swept under the rug fairly often without incident.

Using the restraints, D-0930 began [REDACTED], (since confirmed to be an approximation of his previous modus operandi).

Dog rape?

Addendum: Status Report 27/06/2008:

So why are they so hell bent on drilling to this thing? It will become unreachable, but it is, growing? Maybe? Or is it shrinking? Expanding? Too many redacteds, too much not clear for me. Also, why are the people working on this important assignment either not aware of the effects and constantly getting personnel and equipment confused? Once, happens! Twice or more, really?

Sorry, not feeling this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one when it was first submitted in the chat, and I still like it now.

Is it a little bit silly and "out there"? Yes, and that's part of why I like it.

Sure, the Foundation would theoretically approach something like this with the idea that "it's probably memetic or something", but I really like the idea that there's an object out there with an effect strong enough to catch a researcher off guard. Plus, I like the idea that the employees outside the area of effect are becoming increasingly frustrated with the employees inside the area of effect due to their inability to "see things clearly", and vice versa.

I also really like the fact that, every time I read this article, I think, "Wait, why isn't X considered a human?", and then go back and discover some little twist of wordplay revealing that X is in fact considered as such.

Lastly, I actually like the fact that the scip is identified as the concept of humanity in the area of effect, and not the area itself. It gives the article an opening that really caught me by surprise, and I like that, even if it is a little bit nonstandard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What they generally work with is no concern of mine, the foundationverse doesn't bend to the foundation's whims. BUT I see what you're saying in that foundation researchers would prefer to think of this as memetic-or-something rather than an underlying truth. The problem is that they can't be accurate and think it's memetic at the same time. I chose to take the angle that they would go for accuracy, even if it conforms to the skip's "wishes." Unfortunately, their need for accuracy gets in the way of their attempts to reach the source of 2025.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, can't believe I missed that.

"Probably memetic or something" is just a phrase that I used to describe weird brain shit. I think the Foundation would probably think about it more as "a place that makes people think moving stuff is human" rather than "a place that makes moving stuff metaphysically human". I'm pretty sure that's the bit that's pushing people towards a downvote; the Foundation generally doesn't work with deep metaphysical truth, they work with things that make you think wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you not catch the location in the logs being indicated as "onboard Ms. Twain"?

As for why it's not memetic: I tend to think memetics can be treated with amnestics and are infectious. This can't be treated and doesn't propagate outside its area. But maybe that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, but I agree with Mortte in that the Foundation would come at it with the mindset of "It's probably memetic or something" rather than "Oh man this changes things into HUMANS wow!"

Also, the ship should probably be talked about as a person when it's in the area. It's capable of movement, and probably has an autopilot. Most large ships do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, what the hell, SCP Wiki voters. There's nothing significantly wrong with this article and quite a lot right - and, come on, those little side references to recordermen and surveillancemen?

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm quite tired today, so I might just be being dopey, but it took me way too long to understand what this did, exactly. The fact that it took me until 'black and brown-spotted human' makes me think it's probably just me. Nevertheless, I just don't like this overall - if feels too long and cluttered for what it is, and what it is isn't even that interesting. -1 Downvote rescinded and replaced with an upvote. 2014-me was not a particularly smart article-reader, clearly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I'd get down and dirty with the abstract. What does it mean to be human? What is the human condition and what does it mean to suddenly have it thrust upon you? What is a creature to do with the humanity that overwhelms what sense of self of their own creaturehood? Does a fish mourn the lack of lungs? Does a shark long for affection? Does the Octopus find their lack of joints disturbing? Or do they gain the sense of something more, do they hope and dream? Do they grieve? Do they despair?

If even feeling inhuman is part of the experience of humanity, how do nonhuman creatures cope? Ask the big questions. See where it leads you. It might result in something beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's what I'm seeing too. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with SoullessSingularity. There is a good idea here, but the writeup comes across as one-note and off-tone.

It seems like the SCP would be the area of affect, not the abstract concept. Also, the effect would be described with regard to how it makes people recognize things as human, as opposed to suggesting that it imbues the metaphysical concept of humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Changed to APD.

My apologies for you not liking the execution. Any ideas on how it might be improved?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really care for the term "extreme sociopathy". Might be better to use like, actual scientific terms like "antisocial personality disorder". I also question what the hell is a moderate case of sociopathy. Or even a mild case.

than that, while I love the concept I dislike the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just kinda seems like a mash-up of the Toaster and the LaBeouf Viewer :\ -1

There's a few amusing lines in there, but I dun like it overall :<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"inhumane treatment of deep-water drilling humans."

+1

item.title: Cognitive dissonance is considered normal
item.content:encoded:
What is man that you are mindful of him?

Thanks for the feedback from #site19, and especially to Zolgamax and Huitzil.

E: I realize this is a little late, but I have changed the article in hopes of reducing its bad.
